title: Interactive work without a server with web assembly
text:
Histogram interacting with a javascript Observable input component:

viewof sex_box = Inputs.checkbox(
  ["M", "F"],
  {
    value: ["M", "F"],
    label: "Shown sexes:",
  }
)






















A more complex examples with more interactive inputs (NB: it seems that when moving the slider the image get re-computed for each intermediate value, leading to some slowness sometimes) :









viewof sex_show = Inputs.radio(["M", "F"], {label: "Sex:",value:"F"})

viewof year = Inputs.range([1880, 2010], {step: 10, label: "starting year:"})

viewof n_names = Inputs.range([1, 12], {step: 1, label: "number of top names:"})

title: Analysis
text:
We load the necessary packages for our analysis.

library(babynames)
library(knitr)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(pheatmap)

We check the top 10 rows of the dataset with baby names, and show them as a table using kable function.

head(babynames) |> 
  kable()




year
sex
name
n
prop




1880
F
Mary
7065

The function get_most_frequent gets the most frequent baby names over a time-period, whereas the function plot_top plots the top n most popular names.


Code
get_most_frequent <- function(babynames, select_sex, from = 1950) {
  most_freq <- babynames |>
    filter(sex == select_sex, year > from) |>
    group_by(name) |>
    summarise(average = mean(prop)) |>
    arrange(desc(average))
    
  return(list(
    babynames = babynames,
    most_frequent = most_freq,
    sex = select_sex,
    from = from))
}

plot_top <- function(x, top = 10) {
  topx <- x$most_frequent$name[1:top]
  
  p <- x$babynames |>
    filter(name %in% topx, sex == x$sex, year > x$from) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = year, y = prop, color = name)) +
    geom_line() +
    scale_color_brewer(palette = "Paired") +
    theme_classic()
  
  return(p)
}


Plot the figure for the top 10 most popular girl names.

get_most_frequent(babynames,
                  select_sex = "F") |>
  plot_top()







Figure 1: Line plot showing the top 10 most popular girl names from 1950 to 2020





Figure 1 shows the plot of the 10 most popular girl names from 1950 to 2020 in the US, such as Lisa, Jennifer, and Jessica.
Now we plot the figure for the top 10 most popular boy names.

get_most_frequent(babynames,
                  select_sex = "M") |>
  plot_top()







Figure 2: Line plot showing the top 10 most popular boy names from 1950 to 2020





Figure 2 shows the plot of the 10 most popular boy names from 1950 to 2020 in the US, such as Michael, James, and David, and how these names’ popularity decreases around 1990.

title: About
text:
This site details a small analysis of the babynames dataset.






Note



Note that there are five types of callouts, including: note, warning, important, tip, and caution.








TipTip with Title



This is an example of a callout with a title.








CautionExpand To Learn About Collapse





This is an example of a ‘folded’ caution callout that can be expanded by the user. You can use collapse="true" to collapse it by default or collapse="false" to make a collapsible callout that is expanded by default.

title: Tutorial
text:
In this project we aim to visualise the trends of the most frequently used baby names from 1880 to 2017 in the United States. We do this by:
